input_path: data\papers\The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin (PT)** system under study is a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)-based incubator**, designed to regulate and maintain a target temperature within an insulated container. The system comprises the following key components:
  - **Insulated Container**: A styrofoam box providing thermal insulation to minimize heat loss.
  - **Heating Element**: A heatbed responsible for generating and supplying thermal energy to the container.
  - **Air Circulation**: A fan to ensure uniform temperature distribution inside the box.
  - **Sensors**:
    - Two **internal temperature sensors** for real-time monitoring of the internal environment.
    - One **external temperature sensor** to measure ambient conditions outside the container.
  - **Control Unit**: A **Raspberry Pi** acting as the central controller for processing sensor data and executing control actions (e.g., adjusting heating power).
  The system operates in a **closed-loop control domain**, where the goal is to achieve and sustain a predefined temperature setpoint regardless of internal content variations or external environmental disturbances. Its suitability for **Digital Twin (DT) implementation** stems from its **dynamic, data-driven, and feedback-dependent** nature, requiring real-time monitoring, predictive modeling, and adaptive control to ensure robustness, safety, and efficiency.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** of the incubator provides the following **specific services** to users and the physical system, leveraging **modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimization** technologies:
  - **Real-Time Monitoring**: Continuous acquisition of sensor data (internal/external temperatures) from the **Physical Twin (PT)** via embedded sensors, enabling **state estimation** and anomaly detection.
  - **Predictive Simulation**: Execution of **dynamic models** (e.g., differential equation-based simulations) to replicate and predict the **thermal behavior** of the PT under current or hypothetical operating conditions, accounting for factors like heat transfer, sensor noise, and environmental variability.
  - **Calibration & Model Refinement**: Use of **calibration algorithms** to iteratively update **cyber-physical models** (e.g., thermal response models) by comparing simulated outputs with real-time sensor data, ensuring high-fidelity representation of the PT’s behavior.
  - **Safety & Performance Validation**: Deployment of **monitoring algorithms** (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers) to assess whether the PT operates within **safe and optimal bounds** (e.g., temperature thresholds). Violations trigger **corrective actions** (manual or automated) to adjust the PT’s configuration (e.g., heating power).
  - **Optimization & Control**: Integration of **optimization algorithms** (e.g., state estimators, supervisory controllers) to dynamically adjust control parameters (e.g., fan speed, heatbed power) for **energy efficiency, robustness, and minimal overshoot/undershoot** during temperature regulation.
  - **Visualization & Decision Support**: Generation of **advanced visualizations** (e.g., time-series plots, 3D thermal maps) to facilitate **engineering communication**, root-cause analysis, and **what-if scenario testing** (e.g., impact of sensor failures or environmental changes).
  **Target Users**:
    - **Engineers**: For system analysis, debugging, and model refinement.
    - **Operators**: For real-time monitoring and intervention during anomalies.
    - **Maintenance Teams**: For predictive maintenance and failure prognosis.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** implementation for the incubator leverages the following **specific tools, technologies, and enablers**, categorized by their roles:
  - **Hardware Platform**:
    - **Raspberry Pi**: Acts as the **embedded controller** for the PT, interfacing with sensors and actuators (e.g., heatbed, fan).
  - **Software & Development Tools**:
    - **Python-Based Framework**: Likely used for **model implementation, calibration, and algorithm development** (inferred from the repository’s focus on CPS and DT).
    - **GitHub Repository**: Hosts **up-to-date hardware/software specifications** ([INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)), including **sensor configurations, control logic, and model parameters**.
  - **Simulation Engines**:
    - **Differential Equation Solvers**: Employed to simulate **thermal dynamics** (e.g., heat transfer, system response to control inputs) with varying levels of abstraction (trade-off between accuracy and computational speed).
  - **Data & Model Management**:
    - **Time-Series Databases**: Store **sensor data streams** for historical analysis and model calibration (e.g., InfluxDB or similar, though not explicitly named).
    - **Calibration Algorithms**: Implement **parameter estimation techniques** (e.g., least squares, Bayesian inference) to refine models using real-time data.
  - **Control & Optimization Algorithms**:
    - **Supervisory Controllers**: Enforce **safety constraints** (e.g., max/min temperature limits) and trigger corrective actions.
    - **State Estimators**: Use **sensor fusion** (e.g., Kalman filters) to mitigate noise and improve temperature state accuracy.
  - **Visualization Tools**:
    - **Matplotlib/Plotly**: Likely used for generating **interactive plots** of temperature trajectories, model predictions, and anomaly alerts (inferred from common DT visualization practices).
  - **Interoperability Enablers**:
    - **Modular Architecture**: Supports **reconfigurability** (e.g., swapping models or controllers) to adapt to changing environmental or operational conditions.
    - **APIs/Protocols**: Enable **seamless integration** between PT sensors, DT components, and external systems (e.g., MQTT for IoT data transmission, though not explicitly specified).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system for the incubator case study operates with a **real-time synchronization mechanism** between the Physical Twin (PT) and its Digital Twin (DT) via sensor data acquisition. The system collects **temperature data** from two internal sensors and one external sensor at a **high temporal granularity**, though the exact frequency is not specified in the documents. The DT employs **calibration algorithms** to update its models (e.g., differential equation-based thermal models) with the latest sensor data, ensuring the DT's predictions align with the PT's current state. The latency requirements are implied to be **low** to enable real-time monitoring and control adjustments, such as fault detection or supervisory controller interventions when deviations (e.g., unsafe or suboptimal temperature conditions) are detected. The DT’s temporal granularity is dictated by the **sampling rate of the sensors** and the **computational overhead of the calibration and simulation processes**, with a trade-off between accuracy and simulation speed (e.g., more detailed models with four free parameters are slower but more accurate).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The incubator Digital Twin system is designed as a **single-instance DT** for the Physical Twin (PT), with no explicit mention of multiple DT instances or decentralized architectures. The system follows a **centralized architecture**, where the DT integrates **data** (from sensors), **models** (thermal dynamics of the heatbed, fan, and insulated container), and **algorithms** (calibration, monitoring, and control) into a unified framework. The scope of responsibility is confined to the **thermal regulation of the incubator**, with no hierarchical or distributed coordination mechanisms described. The DT’s models (e.g., two-parameter vs. four-parameter thermal models) represent **alternative abstractions** of the same PT, but these are not multiplicities of DT instances. The system’s simplicity precludes complex coordination between twins, as it focuses on a single CPS (Cyber-Physical System) use case.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin constellation for the incubator follows a **monolithic, service-oriented architecture** centered around three core components: **data acquisition** (sensor inputs), **model-based simulation** (thermal dynamics), and **algorithm-driven control** (calibration, monitoring, and optimization). The orchestration is structured as follows:
  1. **Data Flow**: Sensor data (temperature) from the PT is streamed to the DT for real-time calibration of the thermal models.
  2. **Model Integration**: The DT employs **two thermal models** (one with 2 free parameters, another with 4) to represent the PT’s behavior at varying levels of abstraction. The four-parameter model is preferred for its accuracy in capturing thermal inertia.
  3. **Algorithm Execution**: Calibration algorithms adjust model parameters to minimize discrepancies between simulated and observed temperatures. Monitors then evaluate the PT’s performance (e.g., safety, optimality) and trigger corrective actions via supervisory controllers or human intervention.
  The system lacks explicit **resource management** (e.g., cloud-edge partitioning) or **distributed coordination protocols**, as it operates as a standalone CPS-based DT. The architecture is **tightly coupled** to the PT’s hardware (Raspberry Pi controller, heatbed, sensors) and software stack, with no mention of external orchestration frameworks (e.g., Kubernetes, microservices).
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) integrates **computational models** and **data components** to represent and analyze the behavior of the Physical Twin (PT). Models include **abstract representations** of the PT's cyber and physical aspects, such as:
  - **Geometric/Structural Models**: CAD-like representations (e.g., car chassis shapes, incubator box dimensions) capturing static physical attributes without dynamic behavior.
  - **Behavioral/Dynamic Models**: Mathematical formulations (e.g., **differential equations** for car dynamics or **thermal simulation models** for the incubator) describing interactions between the PT and its environment. The incubator case study employed **two simulation models** (one with **2 free parameters**, another with **4 free parameters**) to predict internal temperature, with the latter demonstrating superior accuracy in modeling **thermal inertia** of the heatbed.
  - **Analytical Models**: Algorithms (e.g., **fault detectors**, **supervisory controllers**, **state estimators**, **optimizers**) that process data and models to generate actionable insights or corrections.
  **Data sources** originate from **real-time sensor inputs** (e.g., temperature, force, or environmental variables) collected from the PT over time. **Data management** involves **calibration algorithms** that update models with the best-estimate parameters (e.g., adjusting thermal model coefficients based on observed PT behavior). The DT framework also includes **monitors** that validate system performance against safety and optimality thresholds, triggering interventions when deviations occur. Model relationships are hierarchical, with **lower-fidelity models** (e.g., 2-parameter incubator model) serving as foundational abstractions, while **higher-fidelity models** (e.g., 4-parameter model) refine predictions by incorporating additional physical nuances (e.g., heatbed inertia).
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The Digital Twin relies on a **sensing infrastructure** embedded within the Physical Twin (PT) to collect **real-time operational data**. While specific sensor types (e.g., thermocouples, force sensors) are not detailed in the documents, the incubator case study implies:
  - **Measurement Capabilities**: Sensors capture **environmental and system-specific variables** (e.g., **temperature** inside the incubator, **thermal inertia** of the heatbed).
  - **Spatial Distribution**: Sensors are strategically placed to monitor critical interaction points (e.g., heatbed surface, ambient air, or structural components like the incubator box walls).
  - **Sampling Frequencies/Data Transmission**: Not explicitly quantified, but the DT framework assumes **continuous or high-frequency data acquisition** to enable dynamic model updates and real-time monitoring.
  - **Accuracy/Precision**: The **4-parameter thermal model** in the incubator case study suggests high-precision requirements to resolve **thermal inertia**, implying sensors must provide **substantial resolution** (e.g., ±0.1°C for temperature) to validate model fidelity.
  - **Data Collection Mechanisms**: Data is transmitted to the DT via **embedded communication systems** (e.g., the incubator’s software for controller and simulation model integration), enabling **closed-loop feedback** between the PT and its digital counterpart.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The Digital Twin’s **model fidelity** and **validity** are governed by:
  - **Fidelity Requirements**: Models must balance **abstraction** (e.g., CAD for static geometry) and **detail** (e.g., differential equations for dynamics). The incubator study demonstrated that **higher-fidelity models** (4-parameter vs. 2-parameter) improved accuracy in predicting **thermal inertia**, a critical physical property.
  - **Validation and Verification (V&V) Methods**: **Experimental validation** (e.g., comparing simulated vs. real incubator temperatures) and **parameter tuning** (e.g., calibrating free parameters using sensor data) ensure model alignment with PT behavior. The DT framework employs **calibration algorithms** to iteratively refine model parameters based on real-time data.
  - **Uncertainty Sources**: Quantified through **model discrepancy** (e.g., prediction errors in the 2-parameter vs. 4-parameter incubator models) and **sensor noise**. Uncertainty is mitigated via **higher-fidelity models** and **data-driven corrections**.
  - **Error Detection and Correction**: **Monitors** within the DT detect deviations (e.g., unsafe operating conditions) and trigger **corrective actions** (e.g., adjusting actuators or alerting operators). The incubator’s **controller** likely implements **feedback loops** to maintain temperature within specified bounds.
  - **Quality Assurance (QA) Processes**: Continuous **model-data comparison** (e.g., validating thermal predictions against sensor readings) and **algorithm-driven optimizations** (e.g., supervisory controllers) ensure sustained DT accuracy. The open-source incubator hardware/software provides a **verifiable platform** for testing and refining these processes.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The Digital Twin (DT) of the incubator system collects **real-time sensor data** from the Physical Twin (PT) via **two temperature sensors** monitoring the internal box environment and **one temperature sensor** monitoring the external environment. Data flows include **temperature readings** transmitted from the PT to the DT for model calibration and monitoring. The communication is facilitated by a **Raspberry Pi** acting as the cyber-physical interface, aggregating sensor inputs and forwarding them to the DT framework. No explicit mention of **communication protocols** (e.g., MQTT, HTTP, OPC UA) or **transmission frequencies** is provided, but the system implies **continuous or periodic sampling** due to the dynamic thermal regulation requirements. **Data quality assurance** is inferred through the use of **calibration algorithms** (as described in Document 1, Fig. 1) to reconcile discrepancies between simulated and real-world data. **Preprocessing steps** are not detailed, but the DT employs **model updates** (via calibration) to ensure data integrity. **Data integration mechanisms** involve merging sensor inputs with **simulation models** (e.g., differential equation-based thermal models) to generate a unified representation of the PT's state for predictive and diagnostic purposes.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The DT exerts control over the Physical Twin (PT) via **command signals** generated by algorithms (e.g., supervisory controllers, optimizers) to regulate the incubator's temperature. These commands are executed by the **Raspberry Pi**, which interfaces with the **heatbed** and **fan** to adjust thermal output. The **control message format** is not explicitly specified, but the system implies **closed-loop feedback** where the DT continuously evaluates sensor data (e.g., internal/external temperature) to refine control actions. **Validation and authentication mechanisms** are not detailed, though the use of **calibration algorithms** suggests a form of **parameter validation** to ensure model accuracy before command execution. **Feedback systems** are inherent to the DT architecture, as monitors (e.g., fault detectors) detect deviations from optimal performance and trigger corrective actions. **Conflict resolution** is not explicitly addressed, but the hierarchical structure (DT → Raspberry Pi → PT) implies prioritization of **safety-critical commands** (e.g., overheating prevention) over non-critical adjustments. **Command execution confirmation** is inferred through the **monitoring loop**, where the DT verifies the PT's response (e.g., temperature stabilization) post-command.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DT leverages a **cyber-physical system (CPS) architecture** where the **Raspberry Pi** serves as the primary communication node between the PT and DT. The **network topology** is centralized, with the Pi aggregating sensor data and relaying control commands. **Communication protocols** are not specified, but the system implies **low-latency, real-time data exchange** due to the dynamic thermal regulation requirements. **Bandwidth and latency requirements** are critical for maintaining synchronization between the PT and DT, though exact metrics (e.g., <100ms latency) are not provided. **Reliability and redundancy mechanisms** are not explicitly described, but the use of **multiple sensors** (three temperature sensors) suggests fault tolerance for critical data streams. **Security protocols** are not mentioned, though the DT’s reliance on **calibration algorithms** and **closed-loop control** implies a need for **data integrity** and **authenticated command execution** to prevent adversarial interference.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The DT infrastructure is **edge-deployed**, with the **Raspberry Pi** hosting the core computational workloads (e.g., model calibration, monitoring, control algorithms). **Computational requirements** are modest, as the system relies on **simplified thermal models** (e.g., differential equation-based predictors with 2–4 free parameters) rather than high-fidelity simulations. **Storage requirements** are minimal, focusing on **real-time sensor data** and **model parameter updates**. **Scalability mechanisms** are not addressed, but the modular design (e.g., replaceable models) allows for incremental complexity. The **deployment model** is **on-premise/edge-centric**, with no mention of cloud integration. **Availability/reliability assurance** is implied through the **closed-loop monitoring** and **calibration feedback**, though no explicit redundancy (e.g., backup Pi nodes) or failover protocols are described. The system prioritizes **real-time responsiveness** over high availability, given its safety-critical thermal regulation role.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in the provided context includes the following phases: **1. Modeling Phase**: Abstract representations of the Physical Twin (PT) are created at varying levels of abstraction, such as CAD models (e.g., geometric and structural details of a car chassis) or dynamic models (e.g., differential equations for analyzing forces and movement). These models may range from static (e.g., CAD) to dynamic (e.g., physics-based simulations) and are tailored to specific analysis goals (e.g., understanding behavior, optimization, or fault detection). **2. Integration Phase**: Models are integrated into the DT framework alongside **data** (collected via sensors from the PT over time) and **algorithms** (e.g., calibration, fault detection, supervisory controllers, or state estimators). This phase involves updating models with real-time sensor data to ensure alignment with the PT's current state. **3. Operational Phase**: The DT operates in parallel with the PT, using **calibration algorithms** to refine model parameters based on sensor inputs. Monitors within the DT continuously assess system performance (e.g., safety and optimality) and trigger corrective actions (e.g., adjustments to the PT or algorithmic interventions) when deviations are detected. **4. Evolution Phase**: The DT is designed to adapt to changes in the PT or its environment, leveraging **reconfigurability** and **robustness** to maintain accuracy. This phase may involve iterative updates to models, algorithms, or data pipelines to accommodate new operational conditions or system modifications. **Lifecycle Governance**: The documents imply a governance approach centered on **continuous integration of models and data**, **real-time validation** (via monitors), and **human-in-the-loop decision-making** for critical adjustments. The lifecycle emphasizes preserving models post-deployment to enable ongoing value extraction from the PT.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process for the Digital Twin (DT) in the provided context follows a structured methodology centered on **model-driven engineering** and **cyber-physical system (CPS) integration**. Key aspects include: **1. Development Methodologies**: The DT is built using a combination of **modeling** (e.g., CAD for geometric properties, differential equations for dynamic behavior) and **simulation** to replicate and predict PT behavior under real-time conditions. The process involves **calibration algorithms** to align model parameters with sensor-collected data from the PT, ensuring high-fidelity representation. **2. Quality Assurance Practices**: The DT incorporates **monitoring mechanisms** to validate system performance against predefined safety and optimality criteria. Violations trigger alerts for human or algorithmic intervention, ensuring adherence to operational constraints. **3. Testing and Validation Approaches**: Validation is achieved through **experimental results** (e.g., as demonstrated in the Incubator case study), where models are tested against real-world PT behavior. The Incubator example uses **two temperature sensors** (internal and external) and a **Raspberry Pi controller** to validate model accuracy and control efficacy. **4. Version Control**: While not explicitly detailed, the process implies iterative refinement of models and algorithms based on new data or environmental changes, suggesting a **versioned evolution** of the DT. **5. Evolution Management Strategies**: The DT is designed for **reconfigurability**, allowing adaptation to changing conditions (e.g., environmental factors or PT modifications). This is achieved through dynamic updates to models and algorithms, as illustrated by the **calibration loop** in Figure 1, where sensor data continuously refines the CPS and environmental models. The Incubator case study further exemplifies this with **two distinct models** (likely thermal and control models) built with calibration methods to ensure robustness.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin (DT) provides **decision support capabilities** through a combination of **real-time analytics**, **predictive modeling**, and **supervisory control**. Key components include: **1. Types of Insights Generated**: The DT generates insights such as **system performance metrics** (e.g., temperature regulation in the Incubator case study), **fault detection alerts** (via monitors), and **predictive behavior forecasts** (e.g., simulating PT responses to operational changes). **2. Analytics and Processing Capabilities**: The DT employs **calibration algorithms** to update models with sensor data, enabling **state estimation** and **optimization** (e.g., adjusting control parameters to maintain desired conditions). It also uses **supervisory controllers** to enforce operational constraints and **fault detectors** to identify deviations. **3. Decision-Making Processes**: Decisions are made either **autonomously** (via algorithms) or through **human intervention** when critical violations are detected. For example, in the Incubator, the Raspberry Pi controller may autonomously adjust heating based on sensor feedback, while human operators intervene for non-standard conditions. **4. User Interaction Methods**: The DT supports **visualization tools** (e.g., advanced visualizations of PT behavior) and **monitoring dashboards** to present insights to users. The Incubator case study implies a **hardware-software interface** (e.g., Raspberry Pi + sensors) for real-time interaction. **5. Confidence Measures for Recommendations**: The DT’s confidence in recommendations is derived from **model calibration accuracy**, **sensor data fidelity**, and **validation against experimental results** (e.g., the Incubator’s temperature regulation tests). The calibration loop ensures that model predictions are grounded in real-world data, reducing uncertainty in decision-making.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin (DT) system for the incubator case study incorporates **safety-critical system considerations** through its **monitoring and calibration mechanisms**, as depicted in Figure 1. The DT employs **monitors** that continuously check whether the **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** and its environment are performing **safely and optimally** by leveraging real-time data from sensors (e.g., temperature sensors inside and outside the insulated container). **Fail-safe mechanisms** are implied through the DT’s ability to detect violations (e.g., temperature deviations) and trigger **human or algorithmic interventions** to adjust the physical twin (PT) via the **Raspberry Pi controller**. While the documents do not explicitly detail **cybersecurity protocols** (e.g., encryption, authentication systems) or **access control mechanisms**, the DT’s reliance on **sensor data integration** and **calibration algorithms** suggests a need for secure data transmission and validation to prevent adversarial tampering or spoofing. **Risk assessment approaches** are indirectly referenced through the DT’s role in **predicting system behavior** and **optimizing performance**, though no specific methodologies (e.g., ISO 26262, NIST frameworks) are mentioned. The system’s **reconfigurability** (e.g., adapting to changing environmental conditions) implies robustness, but no explicit **threat mitigation strategies** (e.g., intrusion detection, anomaly detection) are described.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.
2 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


2


Figure 1: Schematic overview of a CPS-based Digital Twins. The letter “C” and “P”
represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**

regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1
block_3_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**Abstract**

thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.
